title: Mole Negro Oaxacan Black Mole

seo.title: Authentic Oaxacan Black Mole Recipe (Mole Negro)
seo.metaDescription: Learn to make authentic Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro) with our traditional recipe. Rich, complex, and deeply flavorful, this Mexican classic is perfect for dinner. Get the step-by-step guide!

h2Headings: What is Mole Negro Oaxacan Black Mole?
Ingredients You Need for Oaxacan Black Mole
Pro Tips for the Best Oaxacan Black Mole
seo.imageAlt: A rich, dark bowl of authentic Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro) served with chicken and rice, garnished with sesame seeds.
seo.imageCaption: Experience the deep, complex flavors of Oaxaca with this authentic Mole Negro recipe, a true culinary masterpiece.

description: Mole Negro, the 'King of Moles', is a deeply complex and intensely flavorful Oaxacan sauce, renowned for its rich dark color and intricate balance of chiles, spices, nuts, and chocolate. This traditional recipe guides you through creating a culinary masterpiece that embodies the soul of Mexican cuisine. Prepare to embark on a flavor journey that will delight your senses and impress your guests.

ingredients.notes: disk, such as Ibarra or Abuelita

instructions.title: Roast and Soak Chiles
instructions.text: Heat a comal or dry skillet over medium heat. Toast the mulato, ancho, pasilla, and chipotle chiles individually for 30-60 seconds per side until fragrant and slightly pliable, being careful not to burn them. Transfer to a bowl, cover with hot water, and let soak for at least 20 minutes until softened. Drain and reserve the soaking liquid.

instructions.title: Toast Spices, Nuts, and Aromatics
instructions.text: In the same skillet with a teaspoon of lard or oil, lightly toast the almonds, peanuts, sesame seeds, raisins, cinnamon stick, cloves, peppercorns, and anise seeds until fragrant. Remove and set aside. Then, add a bit more lard or oil and lightly fry the onion, garlic, roasted tomatoes, tomatillos, plantain slices, and corn tortilla until softened and lightly browned. Remove and set aside.

instructions.title: Blend the Mole Paste
instructions.text: Combine the soaked chiles, toasted nuts and spices, fried aromatics, Mexican chocolate, and dried oregano in a blender. Add 1-2 cups of hot chicken broth (or reserved chile soaking liquid, if clean) and blend until a very smooth paste forms. This may require blending in batches and adding more liquid as needed to achieve a thick, pourable consistency.

instructions.title: Strain for Smoothness
instructions.text: Pass the blended mole paste through a fine-mesh sieve or colander, pressing it with a spatula to extract all the liquid and flavor. Discard any remaining solids. This step is crucial for achieving the characteristic silky texture of an authentic Oaxacan mole.

instructions.title: Cook the Mole Sauce
instructions.text: In a large heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven, heat the remaining 2 tablespoons of lard or oil over medium heat. Carefully pour the strained mole paste into the hot fat. It will sputter, so be careful. Cook, stirring constantly, for about 15-20 minutes until the mole darkens, thickens, and the fat separates slightly, indicating it is well-fried and its flavors have deepened. This is a vital step for flavor development.

instructions.title: Simmer and Season
instructions.text: Gradually add the remaining hot chicken broth to the mole paste, stirring constantly to combine. Bring the mixture to a gentle simmer. Reduce heat to low, cover partially, and let the mole simmer for at least 30-45 minutes, stirring occasionally, allowing the flavors to meld and deepen. If it becomes too thick, add a little more broth. Season with salt and a pinch of sugar to balance the flavors. The mole should be rich, dark, and glossy.

tips: For the deepest flavor, ensure your chiles are properly toasted, but never burned. Burnt chiles will make your mole bitter.
Straining the mole is non-negotiable for that authentic silky-smooth texture. Do not skip this step, even if it takes a little extra time.

Mole improves with age! Make it a day in advance and gently reheat for even more developed and harmonious flavors. Store leftover mole in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 5 days, or freeze for up to 3 months.
article:
Embark on a culinary journey to the heart of Oaxaca, Mexico, where one of the world's most complex and revered sauces reigns supreme: Mole Negro. More than just a sauce, Mole Negro is a symphony of flavors, a cultural icon, and a testament to centuries of culinary tradition. Often gracing celebratory tables, this rich, dark, and utterly unforgettable dish is a must-try for anyone seeking authentic Mexican dinner recipes. While it demands time and patience, the reward is an unparalleled gastronomic experience that will transport your taste buds straight to the vibrant markets and kitchens of Oaxaca.

## What is Mole Negro Oaxacan Black Mole?

Mole Negro, specifically the Oaxacan Black Mole, stands as the undisputed queen among the seven moles of Oaxaca. It’s a masterpiece of balance and depth, a dark, velvety sauce known for its intricate blend of smoky, sweet, spicy, and earthy notes. This isn't a quick weeknight meal; it's a labor of love, a multi-day process for many, involving the careful toasting, grinding, and simmering of dozens of ingredients. Its characteristic deep black color comes from the specific combination of dried chilies – primarily mulato, ancho, and pasilla Oaxaca – which are often toasted to the point of being nearly burnt, along with charred tortillas and sometimes even black sesame seeds.

Historically, mole recipes date back to pre-Hispanic times, evolving with the introduction of new ingredients from European trade. Mole Negro is particularly significant in Oaxacan culture, often prepared for weddings, baptisms, Day of the Dead celebrations, and other major festivities. Each family often has its own cherished recipe, passed down through generations, making every Oaxacan Black Mole a unique expression of culinary heritage. The resulting flavor profile is incredibly complex: a subtle sweetness from fruits like plantains and raisins, a profound nuttiness from almonds, peanuts, and pecans, a hint of bitterness from Mexican chocolate, and an aromatic warmth from spices like cinnamon, clove, and anise. It's truly a sauce that tells a story with every bite.

## Ingredients You Need for Oaxacan Black Mole

The ingredient list for Oaxacan Black Mole can seem daunting, but each component plays a crucial role in building its legendary flavor profile. Don't be intimidated; sourcing these items is part of the adventure!

**The Chilies (the backbone):**
*   6-8 dried Mulato chilies
*   6-8 dried Pasilla Oaxaca chilies (or regular Pasilla if Oaxacan are unavailable)
*   4-6 dried Ancho chilies
*   2-3 dried Chipotle chilies (for a smoky kick)

**Nuts and Seeds (for richness and thickness):**
*   1/2 cup almonds
*   1/4 cup peanuts
*   1/4 cup pecans
*   2 tablespoons sesame seeds
*   1 tablespoon pumpkin seeds (pepitas)

**Spices and Aromatics (the soul):**
*   1 small cinnamon stick (about 2 inches)
*   4-5 whole cloves
*   1/2 teaspoon black peppercorns
*   1/4 teaspoon anise seeds
*   1/2 teaspoon dried oregano (Mexican preferred)
*   1/4 teaspoon cumin seeds
*   1/2 white onion, roughly chopped
*   4-5 cloves garlic, peeled
*   2 medium tomatoes, roughly chopped
*   1-2 tomatillos, husked and quartered

**Fruits and Sweeteners (for balance):**
*   1 ripe plantain, sliced
*   1/4 cup raisins
*   1/4 cup dark Mexican chocolate, chopped (or good quality dark chocolate, 70% cacao)
*   1-2 tablespoons piloncillo (Mexican brown sugar cone) or dark brown sugar

**Thickeners and Liquids:**
*   2 stale corn tortillas, torn into pieces
*   2-3 tablespoons lard or vegetable oil
*   6-8 cups chicken broth (or vegetable broth for a vegetarian version)
*   Salt to taste

## Step-by-Step Instructions for Mole Negro

Preparing Mole Negro is a multi-stage process that requires patience and attention to detail.

1.  **Prepare the Chilies:** Carefully remove stems and seeds from all dried chilies. Heat a dry comal or skillet over medium-low heat. Toast chilies in batches, pressing them down with tongs, until aromatic and slightly pliable (about 30-60 seconds per side). Be careful not to burn them, as this will make the mole bitter. For the Mulato, Pasilla Oaxaca, and Ancho chilies, you want them quite dark, almost charred, to achieve the deep black color. Place toasted chilies in a bowl, cover with boiling water, and let soak for at least 20-30 minutes, or until softened.
2.  **Toast Nuts and Seeds:** In the same dry skillet, toast almonds, peanuts, pecans, sesame seeds, and pumpkin seeds separately until fragrant and lightly browned. Remove and set aside.
3.  **Toast Spices and Tortillas:** Toast the cinnamon stick, cloves, peppercorns, anise seeds, oregano, and cumin seeds until aromatic, about 1-2 minutes. Add the torn corn tortillas to the skillet and toast until very dark, almost burnt, to contribute to the black color.
4.  **Sauté Aromatics and Fruits:** Heat lard or oil in a large pot or skillet. Sauté the chopped onion and garlic until softened. Add the chopped tomatoes, tomatillos, sliced plantain, and raisins. Cook until vegetables are softened and slightly caramelized.
5.  **Combine and Blend:** Drain the softened chilies (reserve some soaking liquid). In a high-speed blender, combine the softened chilies, all the toasted nuts and seeds, toasted spices, toasted tortillas, and the sautéed aromatics and fruits. Add about 1-2 cups of chicken broth to help with blending. Blend until as smooth as possible. This may require blending in batches and adding more broth as needed.
6.  **Strain the Sauce:** This is a crucial step for a silky-smooth mole. Pass the blended mixture through a fine-mesh sieve into a large, heavy-bottomed pot. Use a spatula to press the pulp against the sieve, extracting as much liquid as possible. Discard the remaining solids.
7.  **Simmer the Mole:** Heat the strained mole sauce over medium-low heat. Add the chopped Mexican chocolate, piloncillo (or brown sugar), and remaining chicken broth. Stir constantly until the chocolate and sugar are fully dissolved. Bring the mole to a gentle simmer. Reduce heat to low, cover partially, and cook for at least 1-2 hours, stirring frequently to prevent sticking, until the sauce has thickened and the flavors have melded beautifully. If the mole becomes too thick, add more broth or a little of the reserved chili soaking liquid.
8.  **Season and Serve:** Taste and adjust seasoning with salt. The Oaxacan Black Mole is ready when it has a deep, rich color and a velvety texture.

## Pro Tips for the Best Oaxacan Black Mole

*   **Quality Ingredients Matter:** Since there are so many components, the quality of each ingredient will directly impact the final flavor. Seek out fresh, vibrant produce and good quality dried chilies.
*   **Don't Rush the Toasting:** Each ingredient needs its specific level of toast. Toasting unlocks deeper flavors and aromatics. For the chilies contributing to the black color (Mulato, Pasilla Oaxaca, Ancho), don't be afraid to get them very dark, but stop short of turning them to ash, which would make them bitter.
*   **Blend Until Smooth, Then Strain:** A powerful blender is your best friend here. However, even the best blender will leave some fibrous bits from the chilies and seeds. Straining is non-negotiable for that characteristic smooth, luxurious texture of a traditional Oaxacan Black Mole.
*   **Patience is Key:** This isn't a quick sauce. The long, slow simmer allows all the complex flavors to meld and deepen. The longer it simmers, the better it gets.
*   **Balance the Flavors:** Taste frequently. If it's too spicy, a touch more chocolate or sugar can help. If it's too sweet, a pinch of salt or a splash of lime juice can balance it. The goal is a harmonious blend where no single flavor dominates.
*   **Make Ahead:** Mole Negro tastes even better the next day, after the flavors have had more time to marry. It also freezes exceptionally well, making it a perfect make-ahead dish for entertaining.

## Serving Suggestions for Mole Negro

Traditionally, Oaxacan Black Mole is served with poultry, most commonly chicken or turkey. The rich, complex sauce perfectly complements the mild flavor of the meat.

*   **Chicken or Turkey:** Simmer bone-in chicken pieces (thighs, drumsticks, or breasts) directly in the mole sauce until cooked through and tender. You can also roast or poach chicken separately and then smother it in the warmed mole.
*   **Rice and Tortillas:** Always serve your mole with a side of fluffy white rice to soak up every drop of that glorious sauce, and warm corn tortillas (freshly made if possible) for scooping.
*   **Garnishes:** A sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds, a few slivers of red onion, or a sprig of fresh cilantro can add a beautiful visual and textural contrast.
*   **Enchiladas or Tamales:** Use leftover mole to make incredible enchiladas or as a filling for tamales.
*   **Vegetarian Options:** For a meatless meal, swap chicken broth for vegetable broth and serve the mole over roasted vegetables (like sweet potatoes, plantains, or mushrooms), hearty black beans, or even a generous helping of scrambled eggs.

## Variations and Substitutions

While the classic Oaxacan Black Mole has a distinct profile, there's always room for personal touches:

*   **Chilies:** Stick to the core chilies for Mole Negro, but you can adjust the ratio slightly to your preference for smokiness or heat. For a less intense black color, you might reduce the charring of some chilies, but this will alter the traditional appearance.
*   **Nuts and Seeds:** While the traditional mix is recommended, you can experiment with other nuts like cashews or pecans for different textural and flavor contributions. Just ensure they are properly toasted.
*   **Chocolate:** Good quality dark chocolate (70% cacao or higher) works well if Mexican chocolate is unavailable. Adjust sugar levels accordingly, as Mexican chocolate often contains sugar.
*   **Sweeteners:** Piloncillo is traditional, but dark brown sugar or even a touch of agave syrup can be used. Start with a smaller amount and add more to taste.
*   **Broth:** Chicken broth is standard, but a rich vegetable broth makes this dish accessible to vegetarians without sacrificing much flavor complexity.
*   **Lard vs. Oil:** Lard adds an authentic richness, but vegetable oil or avocado oil are perfectly acceptable substitutes.

Creating Mole Negro is an act of culinary dedication, but the journey through its myriad flavors and aromas is an unforgettable experience. This iconic Mexican dinner recipe is more than just food; it's a taste of history, culture, and the heart of Oaxaca. So, clear your schedule, gather your ingredients, and prepare to create a truly extraordinary dish that will impress and delight.
